parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around a sharp but only partly visible value asymmetry. Buffalo values BLUE highly ($0.70) and RED very little ($0.10), so from an analyst view buffalo should prefer turning RED into BLUE whenever possible and may also be willing to give up RED for GREEN if BLUE trades fail. Bear values both BLUE ($1.00) and RED ($0.60) highly, making bear the strongest overall demander of the scarce privately valuable colors and the least natural seller of BLUE; bear is also the only player who values RED substantially, so bear is the natural destination for RED from buffalo or goose. Goose values GREEN at $0.50 but both RED and BLUE at only $0.10, so goose is the most likely party to part with RED or BLUE in exchange for GREEN, though goose does not know others' exact values and will simply use own surplus calculator. Because GREEN has the same public value for everyone and is a safe medium-value chip, it is the most plausible bargaining currency. The main uncertainty comes from private values and from three-player matching risk: a proposer cannot target a recipient, so any offer attractive to more than one responder may be randomly realized, which discourages overly specific cross-player plans. Persona priors suggest buffalo is relatively disciplined and moderately conciliatory, so buffalo may make cleaner, modestly favorable offers rather than extreme ones. Bear is patient and somewhat selective, so bear may not rush into weak trades and may prefer offers that clearly improve own inventory. Goose has lower search and moderate discipline, so goose may accept straightforward positive-surplus offers rather than engage in ambitious probing. Overall, the likely path is early testing offers, then one or two GREEN-for-low-value-color trades involving goose, with bear mostly preserving high-value BLUE and possibly using GREEN to acquire low-cost RED when favorable.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each color. Round 0 should feature exploration under severe informational uncertainty. Buffalo is most likely to test whether someone will give BLUE for RED or GREEN for RED, since RED is nearly worthless to buffalo. Bear has little reason to sell BLUE cheaply because BLUE is bear's most valuable chip, but bear may try to use GREEN to buy RED because RED is valuable to bear and could be cheap for others. Goose is likely to respond mainly to own calculator and should be receptive to getting GREEN in exchange for RED or BLUE, since both are low-value to goose.

After goose's successful trade, holdings are public: buffalo now has more BLUE and fewer GREEN, while goose has extra GREEN and less BLUE. This makes buffalo even less eager to pay GREEN for BLUE again, but buffalo still has many low-value RED chips and knows from experience that direct RED-for-BLUE demands may be too ambitious. Bear has seen at least one GREEN-based trade succeed and may now make a more attractive GREEN-for-RED offer. Goose, now richer in GREEN, becomes less likely to buy more GREEN and more able to use GREEN to offload remaining low-value chips if useful.

Entering the final round, buffalo has already converted some RED into both BLUE and GREEN and may make one last attempt to improve at the margin. Bear has successfully obtained RED and still strongly prefers to keep BLUE, so bear is unlikely to make a generous BLUE sale; however, bear may continue exploiting GREEN as a purchase currency if inventory allows. Goose has shown willingness to make simple surplus-positive offers but has limited incentive to make complex final-round gambits. With only one round left, proposals should become more payoff-calibrated and less exploratory, but private-value uncertainty still limits precision.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED the most, but they do not know that during play. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, while BLUE is privately heterogeneous: otter values BLUE at $0.40, peacock at $0.50, and cat at $0.70. That makes BLUE the main source of hidden gains from trade, especially for cat as a buyer and otter as a seller. Because RED is worth $1.00 to everyone, offers involving RED are unlikely to be attractive unless the requester gives a lot back; as a result, early RED-for-GREEN or RED-for-BLUE proposals are likely to be too demanding and get rejected. Given the personas, otter is likely to open somewhat cautiously and somewhat anchor off simple even-numbered exchanges, peacock is moderately exploratory and may test multiple exchange rates, and cat is relatively disciplined and likely to notice profitable BLUE acquisition opportunities. The main uncertainty comes from private values and from the three-player acceptance rule: a proposer cannot target a specific responder, so any offer attractive to one player might also be accepted by another and then assigned randomly among acceptors. That randomness discourages highly recipient-specific proposals and favors broadly acceptable offers. Overall, the most likely pattern is some failed exploratory offers in round 0, followed by one or two accepted trades centered on cat acquiring BLUE from otter at rates that are weakly or clearly beneficial to both sides, while peacock remains more of a cautious middle player who can accept fair BLUE-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED opportunities but has less extreme comparative advantage than otter or cat.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning from revealed willingness to trade, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative. Because everyone starts with 10 of each chip, inventory constraints are slack, and senders can test whether others will part with RED or BLUE. But the hidden-value environment means early proposers may overestimate the attractiveness of asking for RED. This makes initial turns more likely to feature rejected feelers before players infer, from responses and accepted trades, which colors seem easier to move.

After round 0, the accepted cat-otter trade publicly reveals that BLUE can move for more than one-for-one against GREEN. That should make BLUE a more salient bargaining object. Otter now has fewer BLUE and more GREEN, cat has more BLUE and fewer GREEN, and peacock has unchanged holdings. Since cat's BLUE valuation is highest, cat is less willing to sell BLUE after acquiring some, while otter remains the natural low-value BLUE seller if the terms are good enough. Players may also infer that demands for RED are hard to place, so proposals are more likely to pivot toward BLUE-GREEN exchanges.

By the final round, the history points fairly strongly toward otter as a willing BLUE seller and cat as the strongest BLUE buyer. Peacock moves first this round and may try one more moderate exchange, but with limited evidence that others will part with BLUE cheaply. Later turns are shaped by updated inventories: otter has repeatedly converted BLUE into higher-valued assets and may now either try to improve RED holdings further or hold back if remaining BLUE is scarce; cat, with disciplined bookkeeping, is likely to continue any still-profitable BLUE accumulation if otter still has inventory. Because this is the last round, there is less reason to preserve optionality, so final proposals are more likely to target immediate positive surplus rather than strategic signaling.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear but imperfectly knowable comparative-advantage structure. Badger values RED and BLUE equally highly at $0.80 and values GREEN only at $0.50, so badger should want to give up GREEN when possible and acquire RED or BLUE. Camel values RED at $0.60, GREEN at $0.50, and BLUE at only $0.40, so camel most wants RED, is roughly neutral toward GREEN, and should be most willing to part with BLUE. Squid values BLUE at $0.60, GREEN at $0.50, and RED at only $0.20, so squid strongly prefers BLUE, is fine with GREEN, and should be very willing to part with RED. The most efficient direct pattern is therefore camel giving BLUE for RED from squid, and badger trying to obtain either RED or BLUE by paying with GREEN. However, each player only knows his own values during play, so proposals must be based on inference from acceptance behavior rather than full surplus knowledge. That creates uncertainty about which counterpart is the right target and how demanding an exchange rate can still get accepted. Because acceptance decisions are made with calculators showing exact private gains and losses, strictly loss-making offers should usually be rejected. The personas suggest camel is the most deal-ready accepter, squid is careful but highly disciplined, and badger is moderately exploratory. That makes early probing likely: badger may test whether others will accept GREEN-for-BLUE or GREEN-for-RED offers; camel may make practical offers to turn low-value BLUE into RED; squid may try to convert low-value RED into BLUE or sometimes GREEN. Across three rounds, accepted trades are most likely when they give the recipient a clear private gain and use inventory the sender can spare. Trade rates may remain somewhat conservative because proposers face two-sided screening uncertainty and cannot choose among multiple acceptors.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no revealed preference data beyond the common chip ranges and their own calculators. That usually encourages mildly exploratory but still positive-surplus offers. Badger is likely to start by offering GREEN, his relatively lower-valued chip, for BLUE, which he values highly. Camel is the most likely early accepter if an offer gives even modest positive gain, while squid should reject trades that cost him BLUE unless compensated generously because BLUE is his best chip. Camel's own proposal is likely to leverage his low-value BLUE to request RED, since RED is his best chip and either badger or squid might accept if the ratio is favorable. Squid then has an incentive to offer RED, his least valued chip, in exchange for BLUE or possibly GREEN, but asking for BLUE too aggressively may fail if the terms are not attractive enough to recipients.

By round 1, everyone has observed that camel accepted GREEN for BLUE and that squid accepted BLUE for RED, revealing something about directional willingness even though not private values. Badger now has evidence that camel may be a useful source of BLUE, while squid may part with RED when paid in BLUE but not necessarily at steep rates. Since badger has already increased his BLUE holdings, he may pivot to acquiring RED with GREEN from squid, who dislikes RED. Camel, having improved his RED position, may continue converting BLUE into RED if possible, but he should avoid paying so much BLUE that the trade becomes negative for him. Squid has learned that neither rival accepted his earlier attempt to get BLUE by giving RED at an unfavorable ratio, so he is likely to soften terms by asking for GREEN instead, because GREEN is worth more than RED to him and can also be privately profitable for recipients who do not value GREEN especially highly.

In the final round, inventories and revealed behavior narrow the likely bargaining space. Badger has repeatedly shown willingness to spend GREEN for high-value colors, and squid has shown willingness to unload RED. Camel remains the most flexible accepter but will only take trades with clear positive value. End-game behavior often becomes slightly more aggressive because there is no future round to preserve reputation for, but the calculator still disciplines choices: recipients should reject any negative-surplus deal. A plausible final sequence is badger attempting one more favorable GREEN-for-BLUE trade with camel, camel then trying a simple BLUE-for-GREEN conversion if he has learned that others may accept small gains, and squid finishing with another attempt to turn RED into GREEN at a rate that at least one opponent might accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around the strong and asymmetric red-chip incentives. Dog values RED most at $1.00 and BLUE poorly at $0.20, butterfly values BLUE most at $0.90 and also likes RED at $0.60, while ram values GREEN most tied with modest RED at $0.40 and values BLUE very little at $0.10. Because GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, it is the natural medium of exchange, but not a source of surplus by itself. The largest two-player gains come from moving RED away from ram toward dog or butterfly, and moving BLUE away from ram or dog toward butterfly. Dog should want to convert BLUE or some GREEN into RED; butterfly should want to convert RED into BLUE only if terms are favorable, but more importantly should try to acquire cheap RED from ram using GREEN; ram should often be willing to part with BLUE for almost anything and may also sell RED for GREEN at favorable ratios. The main uncertainty is informational: players know only their own values, so they must infer from observed offers who likely values what. That creates cautious probing in early turns. Persona priors suggest dog is relatively conciliatory and may make workable rather than maximally extractive offers, butterfly is disciplined and quick-deal oriented so likely to post efficient, clean offers when he sees a positive trade, and ram is exploratory and moderately patient, making him willing to test terms and accept profitable but not necessarily immediate trades. Overall, a plausible path is early probing around GREEN-for-RED, followed by butterfly successfully buying RED from ram, after which dog pushes harder to acquire remaining RED and butterfly later exploits dog's low BLUE value by offering BLUE for RED on terms dog still finds individually profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is likely to be exploratory because nobody has direct knowledge of others' private values yet. Dog moves first and, given her own values, should prefer to spend BLUE or GREEN to buy RED. Butterfly is likely to recognize that GREEN can be used as a neutral currency to test whether someone is willing to sell RED. Ram, moving last, may try a search-oriented offer involving BLUE because BLUE is least valuable to him. Early acceptance decisions should be driven tightly by own-side calculator gains.

By Round 1, public history suggests ram was willing to sell RED for GREEN and to unload BLUE cheaply, while butterfly showed interest in acquiring RED. Dog can infer that RED may be obtainable from ram and should target it more aggressively. Butterfly now has extra RED from ram and may hold it because RED is still worth $0.60 to him, unless he can trade it into highly valued BLUE at an attractive rate. Ram, after seeing acceptance for BLUE-for-GREEN, may continue offering BLUE away but could also preserve his now lower RED stock.

In the final round, incentives sharpen because there is no continuation value. Dog has already improved by buying RED and may still seek one more favorable conversion from low-valued BLUE or excess GREEN into RED. Butterfly, being quick-deal oriented and disciplined, is likely to post a clean final attempt to buy BLUE with RED, especially after observing dog repeatedly take BLUE-related deals and therefore plausibly not valuing BLUE highly. Ram may continue a straightforward profitable BLUE sale if inventory remains. Endgame uncertainty is lower because trade history has revealed partial preference information through behavior.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around mutually beneficial red-blue swaps, because player values are sharply opposed on those two colors while green is a flat $0.50 for everyone. Lion values BLUE most ($1.00) and RED relatively little ($0.40), so lion prefers to give away red and acquire blue. Iguana values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.40), so iguana prefers to give away blue and acquire red. That creates the clearest two-sided gains in the game. Alligator has milder preferences, valuing BLUE at $0.60 and RED at $0.30, so alligator also likes moving red out and blue in, but with weaker gains than lion and without knowing that lion values blue so highly. Because players know only their own values during play, proposals must be attractive under uncertainty: a sender can infer that offering green may be safer because green is never terrible, but green does not create as much surplus as red-blue exchange. The main uncertainty is whether proposers test ambitious terms first, hoping someone accepts, or quickly move toward balanced-looking rates. Given the personas, lion is likely to search for efficient trades and accept positive-surplus offers without excessive guardedness; alligator is somewhat more cautious and structured, likely to make reasonable rather than wild offers; iguana is moderately exploratory and should accept clear gains. Overall, the most likely path is early probing, some declined offers due to asymmetric private values, and then accepted red-for-blue trades, especially between lion and iguana, with possible follow-up trades involving alligator once holdings shift.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each color, so inventory constraints are loose and players can test the market. Because no one knows others' valuations, early proposals are likely to be informative probes. Alligator's best own-side move is to trade away red for blue, but asking too aggressively may fail because others may not want to part with blue. Lion and iguana are the pair with the strongest latent complementarity, but they do not know that yet, so the first round likely includes at least one declined exploratory offer before a clearer positive-surplus trade appears.

After round 0, the public history reveals that lion and iguana are both willing to trade blue against red, which gives alligator some information even without knowing exact values. Lion has accumulated more red and slightly reduced blue, making another red-for-blue trade still attractive. Iguana has moved further toward red-heavy holdings and may continue exploiting that pattern. Alligator is likely to adjust toward a more acceptable exchange rate than the initial probe, but because alligator's gains from trade are smaller than lion's, acceptable terms may still be too demanding for others.

By the last round, players have more evidence from observed acceptances and updated holdings. Red-blue exchange has been validated publicly, while green-based offers still look less targeted and less likely to clear. Final-round behavior often becomes slightly more concrete: senders can use holdings to craft feasible trades that are good for themselves and plausibly acceptable to at least one counterparty. Lion and iguana remain the strongest trading pair, while alligator may try one more moderate swap using the now-established red-for-blue pattern.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-for-blue exchange, with green playing only a minor balancing role. From an analyst view, whale-red-86 and duck-orange-4971 both value RED highly at $1.00, while dolphin-orange-3122 values BLUE most at $0.80 and RED less at $0.60. That creates the clearest gains from trade when dolphin gives BLUE to obtain RED from either whale or duck. Whale values BLUE only $0.40, so she is the natural seller of RED if offered enough BLUE. Duck also values RED more than BLUE, so duck is less eager to sell RED than whale, but may still accept favorable terms. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green-for-anything trades only work when used as a quantity-adjustment device; it is not the main source of surplus. The strategic uncertainty comes from private values during play: dolphin does not know exactly who values RED least, and whale and duck do not know how much dolphin values BLUE. Because accepted trades can be claimed by any accepting player at random, proposers also face targeting risk and may avoid offers that more than one counterpart would like. Persona priors suggest whale is relatively conciliatory and adaptive, dolphin is low-search and may make straightforward rather than highly optimized offers, and duck is fairly deal-ready with a low acceptance threshold. Overall, the most likely path is that early offers test the market, then dolphin pushes to convert BLUE into RED, while whale tries to use her low-value BLUE to acquire more RED when possible but struggles because nobody else strongly wants RED less than she does except dolphin.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each color, so no inventory pressure exists yet. The first round is likely to be exploratory. Whale, who values RED most and BLUE least, has the strongest internal motive to trade away BLUE for RED, but because others also value RED fairly highly, an aggressive ask for RED is unlikely to be accepted. Dolphin is the player with the clearest outward trading role because BLUE is especially valuable to him and RED is relatively cheap, so he may probe whether others will part with RED in exchange for BLUE. Duck is cooperative and fairly acceptance-ready, but with limited search she may make a simple, moderate offer rather than a finely tuned one.

After round 0, whale has fewer RED and more BLUE, making her even more motivated to reverse that shift if possible. Dolphin now has extra RED and less BLUE, so he may continue converting BLUE into RED only if he still sees profitable opportunities, but he also becomes a potential seller of RED for sufficiently attractive terms. Duck has not traded and remains flexible. Because public holdings are visible, players can condition on who still has inventory in the relevant color. The strongest feasible deals still involve dolphin obtaining RED for BLUE, but whale may also test whether dolphin will part with some newly acquired RED for a package involving BLUE or green.

By the final round, players can no longer benefit from waiting, so acceptable positive-surplus deals are more likely to clear if they are simple and individually profitable. Whale still prefers ending with more RED and less BLUE. Dolphin still values BLUE highly but may willingly use some BLUE to get RED if the exchange rate is favorable from his perspective. Duck, who values RED strongly and has a low acceptance threshold, may accept small profitable opportunities but is unlikely to surrender RED cheaply. Final-round proposals therefore likely become cleaner and more surplus-focused, with one more red-for-blue trade and at least one declined attempt when a proposer asks for too much relative to counterpart incentives.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should center on red-blue exchange rather than green. Green is worth exactly $0.50 to everyone, so it creates little private-information advantage and mostly serves as a neutral filler. The major gains from trade come from differences in red and blue values: turtle-yellow-8674 values BLUE most highly ($1.00) and also likes RED ($0.90), gorilla-purple-9767 values RED most highly ($1.00) and values BLUE moderately ($0.70), and zebra-blue-1097 values both private-value colors least (RED=$0.60, BLUE=$0.50). As a result, zebra is the most natural source of either red or blue chips, because zebra can profit from giving up those chips for enough units of another color, especially green or favorable rates in the other private-value color. Turtle and gorilla each strongly prefer one private-value color and may try to acquire it cheaply from zebra or from each other. However, because each player only knows their own values during play, they cannot directly target the true lowest-value holder with certainty. They must infer willingness from acceptance behavior and public inventory changes. The UI surplus calculator means acceptances should usually happen only when the recipient gets nonnegative or positive value on their own side, so blatantly losing offers are unlikely to be accepted. Turtle's low-search, quick-deal orientation suggests early practical offers rather than elaborate experimentation. Gorilla's more conciliatory, exploratory style suggests offers that are more likely to clear, especially after observing earlier responses. Zebra's prosocial and closure-seeking profile suggests some willingness to accept reasonable positive deals and to make simpler, easier-to-understand proposals, but zebra's lower bookkeeping discipline makes exact ratio optimization less likely. Overall, the likely path is early probing, followed by at least one accepted red-for-blue trade once preferences are partially revealed through behavior, with some later friction because inventories shift and each proposer still faces uncertainty about who will accept.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone starts symmetric in holdings, so bargaining is driven entirely by private values and uncertainty about others' willingness to trade. Turtle is likely to open with a straightforward attempt to turn less-valued red into highly valued blue. Gorilla may then respond with a more executable offer, possibly using blue to buy red, since blue is only moderately valuable to gorilla. Zebra, with the weakest attachment to both red and blue, is the most likely early accepter of a reasonable offer and may also make a simple proposal aimed at converting low-value blue into green or red.

After round 0, gorilla holds more red and fewer blue, while zebra holds extra blue and less red. That public history makes it more plausible to everyone that gorilla likes red and zebra is willing to part with it for a good enough rate. Turtle still wants blue most and may now aim directly at zebra's enlarged blue inventory. Gorilla, now richer in red, has less need to buy more and may try a lighter exploratory trade. Zebra may continue seeking simple positive-surplus conversions, but with some caution after seeing prior rejections.

By the last round, observed trading has made comparative preferences easier to infer from behavior even if not fully known. Turtle has successfully increased blue and may still accept profitable blue-for-red opportunities. Gorilla already converted blue into red earlier and is likely to protect that improved position unless offered a clearly positive final trade. Zebra has acted as the most flexible trader and may continue to monetize low-value chips, but last-round proposals can become more opportunistic because there is no future penalty for failed probing. This makes a mix of one more accepted value-creating trade and some final declined attempts plausible.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around mutually beneficial red-blue swaps, with green mostly serving as a low-information fallback chip rather than the main bargaining target. Using the analyst-side values, cat-green-4185 values RED most highly and BLUE moderately, so cat most wants to acquire red and is relatively willing to give blue. Zebra-pink-2457 values BLUE most highly and RED least, so zebra most wants to acquire blue and is relatively willing to give red. That creates the clearest bilateral gains in the game: zebra giving RED for BLUE and cat accepting. Iguana-yellow-3262 values RED highly and BLUE poorly, so iguana also likes obtaining red and is especially willing to give blue, but because iguana does not know others' exact values, they face uncertainty about whether asking for red with blue offers will clear. Green has fixed common value at $0.50 for everyone, so green-for-red or green-for-blue proposals only work when they exploit uncertainty about others' private red/blue values or when a player uses green as a conservative medium chip. Given the personas, cat is likely to be state-disciplined and somewhat quick to make workable offers rather than search broadly; iguana is careful, numerate, and somewhat guarded, making calibrated proposals and rejecting weak terms; zebra is somewhat conciliatory and patient, making it plausible that zebra accepts visibly profitable offers rather than holding out. Because responders only accept when their own calculator shows a gain, accepted trades should usually produce private positive surplus for the recipient. The main uncertainty comes from private information: players know only their own values, so they may test offers that are good for themselves but unattractive to others. Overall, the most likely path is that cat and zebra discover profitable red-blue exchange, iguana experiments with blue-for-red requests that may or may not clear depending on price, and later-round inventory changes make cat progressively less willing to part with blue and zebra progressively less willing to part with red.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all three players have symmetric inventories, so round 0 is mainly about price discovery under private values. Cat is the most likely to open with a simple, disciplined proposal aimed at acquiring red by offering blue, because that is cat's clearest self-benefiting direction. Iguana also likes red and dislikes blue, but as a responder may reject weak rates. Zebra, who values blue very highly and red weakly, is the most natural counterparty for any blue-for-red proposal even though cat does not know that for sure. Early proposals may therefore be slightly ambitious but still within a range that can accidentally or strategically match zebra's incentives.

By round 1, one successful cat-zebra trade would make the favorable red-blue pattern more visible in public holdings, even if motives remain private. Cat now has more red and fewer blue, so cat may still seek red but should become more selective about giving up blue. Iguana has observed that simple blue-for-red requests did not clear, which may push toward a slightly sweeter offer or a different chip framing. Zebra, after already giving up some red and gaining blue, still strongly values blue but may ask for it at too favorable a rate from the sender side. This round is likely to mix one more efficient trade with at least one unsuccessful attempt caused by overreaching.

In the final round, inventories and bargaining leverage have shifted. Zebra has already converted several low-valued red chips into highly valued blue chips, so zebra is less desperate and may continue to ask for blue on terms others find too expensive. Cat now has accumulated red and depleted some blue, reducing the attractiveness of further blue-for-red offers; cat may pivot to a conservative green-for-red or similar probe, but those are harder to clear because green has common value and recipients can calculate exactly whether the exchange helps them. Iguana, having completed one favorable trade if the earlier offer cleared, may either stop overreaching or try one last calibrated request. Final-round behavior is likely to show less exploration and more exploitation of the best-known counterpart, but remaining gains from trade are smaller and more inventory-constrained, so declines become more likely.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to center on red-for-blue exchange, because green is worth the same ($0.50) to everyone and therefore creates little private-information advantage. Bear values RED and BLUE equally at $0.80, peacock values RED slightly more than BLUE ($0.80 vs $0.70), and swan values BLUE much more than RED ($0.80 vs $0.50). That makes the clearest two-sided gains come from swan giving RED to receive BLUE, especially with peacock, who likes receiving RED. Bear can also profitably trade with swan because bear values both red and blue highly while swan dislikes red, but bear has less reason than peacock to target any one color. Since players only know their own values during play, proposals will reflect uncertainty about whether others also benefit. The interface calculator means players should reject offers that are directly loss-making for them, so accepted trades are likely to be individually profitable on both sides. Persona priors suggest moderate patience and some willingness to explore rather than force immediate agreement, so early turns may include probing offers that test whether someone will accept favorable terms. Over three rounds, the most likely pattern is some initial failed exploratory proposals, then one or two accepted red-blue trades once swan's willingness to part with red becomes behaviorally revealed through acceptance decisions or public trade outcomes.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each color. Because nobody knows others' exact values, round 0 is most likely to feature exploratory proposals. Bear, moving first, may test whether anyone will surrender blue for red at favorable terms. Peacock is somewhat more search-oriented and conciliatory, so after observing any rejection, he is likely to make a proposal that still favors himself but is more plausibly acceptable. Swan's strongest private incentive is to give up red in exchange for blue, but she does not know who values red most, so her first proposal may also probe for a profitable red-for-blue exchange.

After round 0, the public history reveals that peacock and swan can successfully trade red for blue. That makes future proposals more targeted. Bear can infer that red-blue exchange is active but still does not know exact private values, so may either imitate the pattern or try a slightly more demanding version. Peacock and swan, now with evidence of mutual compatibility, are especially likely to continue trading if the terms remain profitable. Inventory constraints are still loose, so the main friction is getting the rate right.

By the final round, the revealed pattern strongly favors straightforward, mutually profitable red-for-blue trades with swan as the natural supplier of red. However, final-round proposals can become a bit more aggressive because there is no future relationship to preserve within this game. Bear and peacock may both test whether someone will accept slightly tougher terms, but responders should still decline any proposal that is privately negative. The likely outcome is one more balanced trade and at least one declined attempt when a proposer overreaches.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely driven by a clear value asymmetry around BLUE chips. Monkey values BLUE most highly at $0.90, camel also values BLUE highly at $0.80, and gorilla values BLUE only at $0.30. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so GREEN works as a relatively safe trading currency. RED is low for monkey at $0.20, medium for camel at $0.50, and low for gorilla at $0.30. As an outside observer with all values visible, the main efficient direction is that gorilla should be willing to give up BLUE for GREEN or RED, while camel and monkey are reluctant to part with BLUE unless they get very favorable terms. Monkey is the natural demander of BLUE but must conceal that privately, and camel can also use GREEN to acquire undervalued chips from others. Because players only know their own values, proposals must be robust to uncertainty about others' acceptance thresholds. The simultaneous private acceptance rule also creates competition risk: if a sender posts an attractive offer, either non-sender may accept, and if both accept the partner is randomized. That encourages proposals targeted at broad acceptability rather than fine-tuned bilateral trades. Persona priors suggest gorilla is patient but not highly exploratory, so she may begin with a simple, somewhat self-serving test offer and then adjust modestly. Camel's high bookkeeping and reasoning make him the most likely to notice workable positive-surplus trades quickly and post calibrated offers. Monkey is search-oriented, disciplined, and less aggressive, so he is likely to explore value-improving trades, especially for BLUE, but without extreme demands. That means fox is the clearest seller of RED and buyer of BLUE, while frog is especially unwilling to give up BLUE cheaply. Bat is the most flexible trader because bat is indifferent between red and blue at the margin and has strong bookkeeping/search priors, so bat is well positioned to test terms and adapt after observing reactions. Because each responder sees their own exact gain/loss from a posted trade, proposals that are negative for both potential recipients should be rejected reliably. The main uncertainty is that proposers do not know others' private values, so early offers may be exploratory and somewhat inefficient. Persona priors suggest relatively low aggression overall, with fox especially less guarded and less exploratory, bat more patient and systematic, and frog moderately cautious. As a result, the most likely full-game path is several modest, acceptance-feasible offers rather than extreme demands, with accepted trades tending to move BLUE toward fox only if fox gives enough RED or GREEN, and RED tending to move away from fox. Since all players start symmetric in inventories and there are only three rounds, players may settle for small individually profitable exchanges once they find terms likely to clear.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning from rejection and acceptance patterns because they do not know each other's valuations. Bat, as the most search-oriented and disciplined player, is likely to open with a probing red-for-blue style offer because bat is personally indifferent between those colors and can use the offer to test market demand. Fox then has incentive to move away from low-value RED and seek BLUE, but may make a somewhat conciliatory offer because of low aggressiveness and low acceptance threshold. Frog, who values BLUE most highly, is unlikely to part with BLUE on weak terms and may instead try to acquire RED or GREEN if the price is favorable.

After round 0, the public history suggests that one-for-one red-blue swaps are too demanding for blue holders, while generous offers that compensate blue sellers can clear. Fox has already shown willingness to overpay in RED for BLUE, and frog has shown willingness to spend GREEN to pick up RED. Bat can now condition on these signals and craft a more acceptable offer, likely targeting fox's excess RED. The strategic environment is still noisy because acceptances come from only one realized recipient and players do not observe private values directly, but the trade history now points toward RED being easier to place than BLUE.

By the final round, the likely pattern is clear: fox is a persistent net supplier of RED and demander of BLUE, frog is willing to acquire RED with GREEN when the exchange is not too costly, and bat can profit from relatively balanced red-blue arbitrage because bat values both similarly. Final-round behavior should be somewhat more settlement-oriented because there is no future opportunity to wait for better terms. Still, players only accept individually profitable trades, so any last-turn proposal asking frog to give up BLUE too cheaply should fail. The most likely ending is one more feasible bat-fox red-blue trade, another fox attempt to buy BLUE, and then a conservative frog offer for RED if inventories still allow it.

GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so GREEN mainly serves as a neutral balancing chip rather than a source of surplus. The main strategic uncertainty is informational: Parrot and Turtle do not know that Dog values BLUE so little, and Dog does not know whether the others value RED or BLUE more. Because proposer and responders each see their own exact gain or loss from a posted trade, accepted trades are likely to be individually profitable on both sides, but proposers may start with somewhat demanding offers to test the market. The personas suggest relatively low aggressiveness for Dog and Parrot, so they may not persist with extreme demands for long, while Turtle is somewhat more guarded and selective, making Turtle less likely to accept thin trades. Since multiple acceptances are randomized, proposers also have some incentive to post terms attractive to either of the two other players rather than targeting one partner too narrowly. Parrot, valuing RED at $0.80 and BLUE at $1.00, is likely to prefer turning BLUE into RED when possible, but only if the rate is not too costly. Turtle also values RED less than Parrot does and is somewhat selective, so Turtle may reject marginal deals.

After Dog's successful first trade, holdings matter more. Dog now has more RED and less BLUE, while Parrot has acquired BLUE and lost some RED. This reduces Dog's urgency slightly but still leaves Dog as the lowest-value holder of BLUE. Parrot may recognize from revealed behavior that Dog is willing to exchange BLUE for RED, but Parrot still does not know Dog's exact values. Turtle has seen one trade execute and two fail, so Turtle may become more cautious about proposing anything that relies on others parting with RED cheaply.

By the final round, the strongest easy gains from trade may be partly exhausted, but Dog still benefits from converting BLUE into RED at favorable rates, while Parrot and Turtle continue to like BLUE. Final-round behavior may become slightly more concessionary because there is no future opportunity value to waiting. At the same time, responders still reject any trade that is directly loss-making on their own calculator, so final agreements should remain clearly positive for both sides rather than purely strategic.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has unusually clear gains-from-trade because red and blue chips have sharply different private values across players, while green is fixed at $0.50 for everyone. Peacock values RED most highly ($1.00) and BLUE least ($0.10), unicorn values BLUE most highly ($1.00) and RED relatively weakly ($0.40), and badger likes BLUE most ($0.90) with moderate value for RED ($0.60). So the strongest efficient pattern is peacock trying to acquire RED, while unicorn and badger are natural sources of RED in exchange for BLUE or sometimes GREEN; meanwhile badger also wants more BLUE but cannot directly buy from unicorn by offering RED at very generous rates because unicorn does not value RED much. Because players know only their own values during play, proposals are made under uncertainty about what others will accept, but the on-screen surplus calculator means each responder will accept only if the offer is privately profitable. That creates a selection environment in which proposals that accidentally align with another player's values can succeed immediately, while aggressive exploratory offers are likely to be declined. Persona priors suggest peacock is the most disciplined and likely to craft calibrated, mutually acceptable offers; unicorn is relatively conciliatory and less holdout-prone, especially on profitable deals; badger is more anchor-sensitive and lower-search, so likely to make simpler, somewhat intuitive offers and adjust only modestly across rounds. Overall, the likely game features peacock successfully converting low-value BLUE into high-value RED, unicorn sometimes converting low-value RED into high-value BLUE, and badger struggling more to source BLUE on favorable terms.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and proposals mainly reflect valuation asymmetries. Early offers are likely to test the market. Badger moves first with limited search and only private knowledge, so a somewhat optimistic attempt to get BLUE cheaply is plausible. Peacock, with strong bookkeeping and the clearest comparative advantage, is likely to propose a trade exchanging BLUE for RED because BLUE is nearly worthless to him and RED is his most valuable chip. Unicorn then has good reason to seek BLUE using RED, since that is a strong private gain if someone accepts.

After round 0, peacock has increased his RED stock and reduced BLUE, badger has gained RED while giving up some BLUE, and unicorn has rebuilt BLUE while shedding RED. Public history now reveals that peacock is willing to buy RED with BLUE and that unicorn can profitably offer RED for BLUE. Badger may infer that BLUE is expensive to obtain and switch toward trying for a smaller, more acceptable exchange. Peacock is still incentivized to buy more RED if possible, but now his remaining BLUE inventory is lower, encouraging a more measured offer. Unicorn, with a shorter horizon and conciliatory style, may continue making straightforward profitable offers rather than holding out.

By the final round, players have more information from observed accepted deals, though not from direct knowledge of valuations. Peacock has likely established that badger is the more responsive counterparty for RED-for-BLUE exchanges. Badger now holds substantial RED and less BLUE, so his final proposal may again try to recover BLUE, but accepted terms still require giving counterparts something they value enough. Peacock in the last middle turn is likely to spend remaining BLUE efficiently for one more RED purchase. Unicorn, moving last, may still seek BLUE with RED if inventories allow, but because badger's BLUE stock has been depleted and peacock values BLUE very little, a final unicorn offer may fail.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around BLUE chips, because BLUE is the only clearly heterogeneous chip: owl values BLUE at $0.70, mouse at $0.40, and dog at $0.30. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, and RED is worth $0.50 to dog and owl but only $0.40 to mouse. That creates the cleanest gains from trade when owl gives up RED or GREEN to acquire BLUE from dog or mouse, and when mouse gives up RED in exchange for GREEN or BLUE only at favorable ratios. However, players do not know others' private values, so proposals must be formed under uncertainty from observed acceptances and rejections. Because all players are described as relatively non-aggressive and somewhat conciliatory, the game is unlikely to feature extreme exploitative offers throughout; instead, early turns should test whether simple one-for-one or slightly sender-favoring swaps clear. Dog's low search tendency makes dog especially likely to open with a straightforward, easy-to-evaluate trade rather than a complex probing sequence. Mouse is a relatively deal-ready accepter and decent bookkeeper, so mouse is likely to accept any visibly positive offer and may make practical proposals that are likely to be taken. Owl has the strongest bookkeeping and the strongest private incentive to accumulate BLUE, so owl is the player most likely to adapt after seeing history and to target BLUE repeatedly. The key uncertainty is that owl cannot be sure why others reject or accept: a rejected BLUE-for-RED swap could mean the ratio is bad, or simply that the wrong recipient saw no gain. Since multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers also have to worry that a broadly acceptable offer might be captured by the less useful counterparty. Overall, the most likely path is modest early experimentation, at least one accepted trade involving owl obtaining BLUE, and later turns adjusting to changed inventories and revealed willingness to trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip, and no behavioral information has yet been revealed beyond the public rules. Dog moves first and, given low search and low aggressiveness, is likely to try a simple trade that looks reasonable from dog's own values, especially something that converts lower-valued BLUE into equally valued RED or GREEN. But because dog does not know owl highly values BLUE, dog may not immediately exploit that. Mouse then gets a chance to react to any revealed acceptance patterns and is likely to make a practical, modest offer rather than an extreme one. Owl closes the round and has the strongest incentive to pursue BLUE once any evidence suggests others might part with it.

By round 1, the history suggests that dog is willing to give up BLUE for RED at one-for-one, mouse was willing to move RED for GREEN at one-for-one, and owl has already shown some appetite for BLUE. Holdings now matter: dog has fewer BLUE and more RED, owl has more BLUE and less RED, and mouse has slightly more RED and slightly less GREEN. Players can infer preferences imperfectly from behavior. Dog may now test whether BLUE can still be converted into GREEN, another equally valued chip from dog's perspective. Mouse, as a low-threshold accepter and reasonably careful bookkeeper, is likely to seek a low-risk positive trade using RED, the chip mouse values least jointly with dog and owl. Owl may become a bit more ambitious in trying to buy BLUE with GREEN or RED, but still needs an offer recipients personally like.

In the final round, players have more information from observed choices. Dog has seen repeated evidence that owl is often the player taking BLUE-related offers, but dog still only knows own payoffs directly and may continue making simple, calculator-supported offers. Mouse has learned that owl will sometimes surrender BLUE only when compensated enough, and may avoid overpaying. Owl has learned that buying BLUE is feasible mainly from dog, but only at ratios dog perceives as clearly beneficial. Because there is no future beyond this round, holdout motives are weaker and any personally profitable trade becomes more attractive. The likely ending is one more accepted exchange with dog as the natural source of BLUE, while at least one final offer still fails because private-value asymmetries prevent mutual gain at the proposed ratio.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely features relatively smooth bargaining because value differences are small and mostly aligned. All three players value GREEN identically at $0.50, while alligator-orange-6315 and buffalo-purple-4069 have identical private values for RED ($1.00) and BLUE ($0.70), and rabbit-yellow-6681 has slightly lower values for RED ($0.90) and BLUE ($0.60). That means rabbit is the comparatively cheapest source of RED and BLUE, while GREEN is the natural payment medium because it has the same value for everyone. From an analyst view, mutually beneficial trades are easiest when rabbit gives RED or BLUE in exchange for enough GREEN, or when rabbit is offered GREEN for RED at a rate that is profitable for rabbit but still attractive to the buyer. However, during play the participants do not know one another's exact valuations, so proposers face uncertainty about who will accept. Because accept/decline decisions are aided by an on-screen surplus calculator, blatantly loss-making offers should be rejected reliably, especially by the more state-disciplined players. Alligator looks likely to be organized and willing to accept positive-sum trades without much fairness resistance. Buffalo is more conciliatory but somewhat more cautious and anchor-sensitive, so early proposals may be shaped by simple exchange-rate intuitions. Rabbit is search-oriented, numerate, and not highly guarded, making it plausible that rabbit explores trades that monetize slightly lower RED/BLUE values into GREEN. Overall, the most likely path is a first round with some testing or mild overreach, followed by convergence on GREEN-for-RED or GREEN-for-BLUE trades involving rabbit as counterparty. Since both alligator and buffalo have the highest RED value, competition for rabbit's RED can also create acceptance uncertainty when an offer is posted to the whole group rather than targeted.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are still inferring what kinds of terms others might accept. Because alligator and buffalo have the same values, their early proposals are likely to be similar in spirit: offer some lower-valued GREEN or BLUE to try to obtain RED. Rabbit is the most plausible seller of RED because RED is worth slightly less to rabbit than to the others. But since nobody knows that for sure in-game, opening offers may be a bit conservative or poorly calibrated. A declined first offer is plausible as players test the market, after which a clearer positive-sum trade can emerge.

After round 0, public holdings reveal that rabbit was willing to part with RED for GREEN, which gives alligator a useful signal about feasible exchange rates. At this point, GREEN has become even more concentrated with rabbit, and RED has become more concentrated with buffalo. That makes a new GREEN-for-RED trade from alligator toward rabbit especially likely. Rabbit's own failed BLUE-for-GREEN offer in the prior round also suggests that the others do not see BLUE as scarce enough to pay aggressively for, so RED remains the more likely focal chip. The middle round therefore should produce at least one additional accepted RED-for-GREEN trade if terms are favorable enough for rabbit.

By the final round, the trade history makes the broad pattern clearer: rabbit can profitably convert some RED into GREEN, while alligator and buffalo are interested in acquiring RED when the rate is not too steep. Inventories now matter more. Rabbit has already sold several RED and accumulated GREEN, so rabbit may become less eager to continue at earlier terms. Alligator, having successfully acquired RED, may switch to a more demanding offer or try for BLUE instead. Buffalo may still pursue RED but has less informational advantage and may post a rate that rabbit no longer likes. Final-round bargaining is therefore more fragile: players understand the market better, but their remaining inventories and outside options are tighter, increasing the chance of decline.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around red chips. Goose values RED highly at $0.90 and therefore dislikes giving it up, while buffalo also values RED relatively highly at $0.80 and bear is comparatively indifferent between red, blue, and green at $0.50 each. Blue creates the clearest disagreement: goose and bear value BLUE at $0.50, but buffalo values BLUE at only $0.20, so buffalo is the player most willing to give blue away and least willing to pay much to acquire it. Under the actual information structure, each player only knows her own values, so they must infer others' willingness from observed behavior. That creates uncertainty, especially early: goose may test whether someone will overpay in blue or green for red; bear may try simple even-looking swaps because all colors are equal to her; buffalo is most likely to propose deals that unload blue for red or green because that is attractive from her own perspective. Because accept decisions are based on each recipient's own calculator, strongly one-sided offers should be rejected reliably. The likely pattern is early probing with at least one declined ambitious offer, followed by trades in which bear parts with red more readily than goose or buffalo would. Personas suggest goose is disciplined and not very exploratory, so she may make straightforward self-favoring proposals rather than many experiments; bear is patient but somewhat selective, so she may decline weak terms and make moderate proposals; buffalo is highly state-disciplined and comparatively conciliatory, making it plausible that she crafts the cleanest acceptable deals once history reveals what is feasible. rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have full inventories and almost no revealed information, so proposals are likely to be tests of the market. Goose has the strongest attachment to red and little reason to sell it cheaply, but she may still post an optimistic red-for-blue request because blue is worth less than red to her and she does not know whether someone else values red less. Bear's equal valuation across colors makes her the most natural source of red if someone offers enough neutral-value chips. Buffalo's key incentive is to move low-value blue out in exchange for red or even green, but an overly aggressive first offer may be rejected if it asks others to give up more value than they receive.

By round 1, one trade has revealed that simple one-for-one exchanges involving green and blue can clear, while red remains hard to obtain. Goose now has slightly more blue and less green, making another blue-acquisition proposal less urgent. Bear still treats colors as substitutes in value terms, so she can continue to be flexible and may start targeting red if she notices others resisting blue-heavy offers. Buffalo has seen that asking for red with blue alone did not work, so a more acceptable proposal would need to sacrifice a chip others plausibly value more, such as green, or ask for less aggressive terms.

In the final round, inventories have shifted enough to sharpen incentives. Bear has already traded away some red and remains the least attached to it, while goose continues to protect red strongly. With only one proposal left per player, there is less reason to posture, but private-value asymmetries still make red costly for goose and therefore difficult for others to buy from her.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip, so nobody is inventory constrained yet. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear value asymmetry that should shape bargaining. Squid values BLUE highly ($1.00) and RED very little ($0.10), so squid's strongest motive is to turn RED into BLUE or at least into neutral GREEN. Badger values both BLUE and RED at $1.00 and GREEN at $0.50, so badger mainly wants to avoid giving up premium chips cheaply and is the hardest player to entice into surrendering RED or BLUE unless the offered quantity is favorable. Camel has more moderate values for the private-value colors, valuing BLUE at $0.80 and RED at $0.40, and is the most deal-ready accepter by persona, so camel is the most likely trading partner for offers that are only modestly profitable. Because recipients see their own exact net gain from each posted proposal, individually loss-making offers should usually be declined. The main uncertainty is that proposers do not know others' values, so they must infer from the public chip ranges and prior acceptances. That creates experimentation: squid is likely to test whether someone will swap BLUE for cheap RED, badger is likely to push for favorable acquisitions of BLUE or to offload GREEN for premium chips, and camel is likely to make simpler, acceptable exchanges that improve his own mix without extreme demands. Across the full game, likely realized trade paths concentrate on deals between squid and camel, because squid can offer RED that is cheap to him but still worth something to camel, while camel can provide BLUE that is valuable to squid yet only moderately costly to himself if the price is good enough. Badger's high value for both RED and BLUE makes him relatively reluctant to accept most incoming offers and likely to propose ambitious terms that others often reject.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and proposals should mostly reveal bargaining posture. Squid likely opens by trying to convert low-value RED into BLUE, but because recipients privately evaluate exact gains, a too-demanding rate is unlikely to clear. Badger then has incentive to test a firm offer because both RED and BLUE are premium to him. Camel, being more acceptance-friendly and less guarded, is likely either to accept a clearly profitable offer or to post a moderate proposal aimed at squid, whose value pattern makes some red-for-blue style exchange plausible.

After round 0, squid now has more BLUE and less RED, while camel has moved in the opposite direction. The observed acceptance tells everyone that at least one counterpart was willing to exchange BLUE for RED at a favorable enough rate, though values remain private. Squid is therefore likely to try another red-for-blue proposal, perhaps slightly more ambitious after seeing one deal clear. Badger still faces the same structural problem: his premium valuation for both private-value colors makes many outgoing offers too aggressive and many incoming offers unattractive. Camel may continue trading if he can earn surplus, but now his BLUE inventory is a little lower and his RED inventory a little higher, so his next proposal may be more conservative or shift toward GREEN.

By the last round, some learning has occurred from acceptances and rejections. Squid has confirmed that camel is the most plausible partner and may make one final attempt to turn low-value RED into something better, but with fewer rounds left there is also less reason to preserve bargaining reputation. Badger is still likely to post a self-favoring proposal because he gains little from balanced swaps involving RED or BLUE, and others can see from their calculators whether his terms are worth taking. Camel, as the final proposer, is likely to choose the easiest positive-surplus deal available rather than a speculative hard bargain, especially if he can still exchange some RED for GREEN with squid.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong underlying gains-from-trade structure, but the gains are highly asymmetric and partly hidden from the players. Ram values RED most ($0.90) and BLUE second ($0.70), so ram is relatively unwilling to part with RED and is most interested in obtaining additional BLUE when the price is favorable. Dog values GREEN most ($0.50), then RED ($0.20), and values BLUE very little ($0.10), so dog has the clearest incentive to trade away BLUE for almost anything else, especially GREEN. Butterfly values RED most ($0.70) and is neutral between BLUE and GREEN at $0.50 each, so butterfly is the most natural counterparty for dog's attempts to unload BLUE, and also may be somewhat willing to exchange BLUE or GREEN in order to obtain RED. The major strategic uncertainty is that each player sees only their own payoffs, so proposers do not know exactly who will find an offer attractive. In addition, if both non-senders would accept, the recipient is random, which discourages proposals that create too much surplus for both opponents. Given the personas, ram is likely to explore and test ambitious terms early, dog is somewhat more conciliatory and likely to make simpler workable offers, and butterfly is disciplined and relatively quick to lock in positive trades. Overall, the most likely pattern is repeated pressure around BLUE-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED exchanges involving dog, with ram probing for favorable BLUE acquisitions and butterfly taking efficient but not overly generous trades.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are weak and the main issue is price discovery under private information. Ram, moving first and being relatively exploratory, is likely to test whether someone will give up BLUE cheaply in exchange for GREEN, a trade ram likes if the BLUE quantity is high enough. Dog then has a strong incentive to move BLUE out of inventory because BLUE is almost worthless to dog, and a modest BLUE-for-GREEN offer is very likely to be acceptable to butterfly, who values BLUE and GREEN equally and may accept a weakly positive or near-neutral deal less selectively than ram. Butterfly, seeing the early history, is likely to make a disciplined attempt to get RED by paying with BLUE, because RED is butterfly's best chip and dog is the player most plausibly willing to release RED.

After round 0, butterfly holds extra BLUE and fewer RED, while dog has gained GREEN and reduced BLUE but still remains the player least attached to BLUE overall. Ram has learned that ambitious asks may fail and is likely to adjust toward a more balanced offer that still improves ram's BLUE position. Dog may continue pursuing GREEN by offering BLUE, but now butterfly may be less eager to accumulate even more BLUE after already taking some. Butterfly, with strong bookkeeping discipline and a quick-deal tendency, is likely to exploit dog's low RED valuation again if a positive RED-for-BLUE swap is still available.

By the final round, players have observed enough history to infer rough directional preferences even without knowing exact values. Ram has seen dog repeatedly willing to take BLUE-for-value-improving swaps and may make one last efficient offer for BLUE. Dog's opportunities are narrowing because both opponents are becoming less interested in absorbing more BLUE unless the terms are clearly favorable. Butterfly, having already improved by adding RED, is less likely to overreach and may instead propose a conservative trade that still has a chance with ram, who values GREEN less than BLUE and RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: The core bargaining structure is driven by asymmetric red and blue values with green fixed at $0.50 for everyone. Lion values RED and BLUE very little ($0.20 each) and values GREEN at $0.50, so lion is naturally a supplier of red/blue and a demander of green. Iguana values BLUE highest ($0.70), RED at $0.50, and GREEN at $0.50, so iguana especially wants to acquire blue cheaply and is roughly indifferent at the margin between red and green. Alligator values BLUE highest ($0.70), GREEN at $0.50, and RED at $0.30, so alligator also wants blue, likes green more than red, and is more reluctant than lion to give up green. This creates one especially strong private-value direction: lion can profitably give red for green, because red is low-value to lion but not to iguana, while green is high-value to lion and only moderate to others. A second likely direction is lion attempting to get blue using red, but that is unattractive to both iguana and alligator because blue is their most valuable chip. Because players only know their own values, proposals must be made under uncertainty about acceptance. The proposer-side calculator makes each player precise about their own gains, so accepted trades should usually be privately profitable for both sides rather than noisy mistakes. Persona priors suggest lion is patient, exploratory, and well organized, making lion likely to test value-extracting offers and adjust after rejection. Iguana is moderately disciplined and patient, likely to make workable rather than extreme proposals. Alligator is somewhat guarded and less exploratory, so likely to reject weak offers and accept clearly positive ones. Overall, the most likely game is one with repeated attempts by lion to turn low-value red into green, with some failed attempts to obtain blue, and with relatively few accepted trades involving blue because both iguana and alligator prize it highly.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has balanced inventories, so bargaining is mainly about exploiting private-value differences rather than stock imbalances. Lion is the player with the clearest arbitrage opportunity and is likely to open by offering red for green, since that is profitable for lion and potentially acceptable to iguana, who values red and green equally. Alligator is less likely to accept such an offer because alligator prefers green to red. After round 0, lion has already improved by converting red into green twice and still has ample red to trade. By the final round, inventories have shifted enough that lion holds more green and fewer red than initially, while iguana and alligator have accumulated some red. The strategic uncertainty is lower because trade history has revealed behavioral tendencies: lion accepts profitable green-for-red deals, iguana is unwilling to part with blue at weak prices, and alligator is selective but open to clear positive exchanges. In a last round, lion is still likely to try one more red-for-green proposal because that has been the most successful route. Iguana may then exploit lion's willingness to swap red and green at near-even value. Alligator, seeing no reason to sell prized blue and no need to force a risky final offer, may make a modest proposal that is too demanding and gets rejected, or simply test a final red-for-green ask that lion now declines if terms are no longer favorable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: The main economic asymmetry in this game is on RED. Whale values RED most at $0.70, dolphin values it at $0.50, and duck values it least at $0.20. That uncertainty should create some failed opening offers, especially from dolphin, whose low search and low reflection cues make rough but simple offers more likely. Whale's profile suggests a somewhat conciliatory but strategically aware approach, so whale is likely to make the clearest acceptable offers for RED. Duck's low acceptance threshold and cooperative style make duck the most likely recipient or accepter when personally profitable, especially on trades where duck gives up RED for GREEN. rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip, so no one is inventory-constrained. Final-round behavior should feature either one last attempt by whale to buy RED efficiently or some conservative proposals that fail because inventories and gains have narrowed. Since there is no future beyond this round, players may accept any strictly profitable deal, but only if the immediate calculator shows a positive private surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear gains-from-trade structure centered on red and blue chips. Zebra values RED most highly ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.30), gorilla has moderate preference for BLUE over RED ($0.40 vs $0.20), and turtle values BLUE most highly ($0.70) and RED least ($0.10). However, players do not know one another's private values during play, so proposers face uncertainty about what ratios others will accept. Because acceptance is individually calculated and non-senders can both accept, a proposer also cannot precisely target a specific partner; if an offer is attractive to more than one player, recipient selection is random among acceptors. That uncertainty discourages very partner-specific offers and encourages proposals that are broadly acceptable or at least likely to be accepted by one side. Persona priors suggest relatively conciliatory exchange styles overall, with zebra somewhat closure-seeking, gorilla exploratory and adaptive, and turtle more quick-deal oriented. rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. Early-round proposals are likely to test acceptance without knowing others' values. Persona priors suggest cat is disciplined but somewhat low-search and may make straightforward value-seeking offers; iguana is highly disciplined and somewhat guarded, likely to test calibrated offers rather than accept thin gains; zebra is more conciliatory and moderately patient, but with weaker bookkeeping may use simpler exchange rates. rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and round 0 is mainly about discovering feasible exchange rates. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around blue chips.
